summary: DHH argues that Twitter and cable news create a distorted picture of public opinion, making loud activist minorities appear to represent mainstream views. He points to several cases — Virginia's gubernatorial election, San Francisco's school board recall, and the Netflix/Spotify controversies — where the silent majority eventually expressed opposing preferences through votes and purchasing decisions. The post contends that most Americans are 'decent and normal' independents who don't engage in online discourse but ultimately wield decisive power. DHH sees this pattern repeating across politics, entertainment, and tech, where brief outrage cycles on social media are followed by the silent majority's contradictory reply through ballots and wallets.
key_points: 75% of Americans never tweet, and independents would be the largest political party, meaning Twitter dramatically misrepresents public opinion
Independent media like Joe Rogan's podcast vastly outperforms cable news, suggesting mainstream media also misreads the public
The Virginia governor's race and San Francisco school board recall showed voters rejecting policies that appeared popular on Twitter
Netflix and Spotify saw no real subscriber losses despite intense online outrage campaigns over Chappelle and Rogan
In competitive markets with low switching costs, consumer behavior is the truest measure of public sentiment — and it consistently contradicts Twitter outrage
money_quotes: Most people you meet in everyday life — at work, in the neighborhood — are decent and normal. Even nice. But hit Twitter or watch the news, and you'd think we were all nuts and nasty.

This reality of 'decent and normal' people not expressing their preferences on the big topics online means they appear silent. Until suddenly they reveal their big stick as a belated reply, and the commentariat is surprised by the force.
In politics they vote with their ballot, in commerce they vote with their wallet.
The loud minority loom impossibly large for a brief moment in history, and then change or not comes on the basis of the silent majority's reply.
Streaming music and streaming video? Two of the most competitive arenas on the internet! Scarcely any friction in hopping from Spotify to Apple Music or from Netflix to Amazon Video.
How many developers moved their projects in protest to GitLab or some other source repository? I can't even recall one.
key_insight: Twitter outrage cycles consistently misrepresent public opinion, and the silent majority's actual reply — delivered through votes and purchasing decisions — repeatedly contradicts the loud online consensus.
